title: Dead Lettering, Poison Messages, and Parking · Systems Transfer
summary: An independent Go integration team receives different topology, traffic, schema, tenant, dependency, and shutdown constraints and transfers the module into a new a Go poison-message remediation workflow. Planned artifact: Produce an independent changed-context version of a Go poison-message remediation workflow with automated and judgment evidence. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: Trace nack or reject without requeue, expiry, length overflow, and quorum delivery-limit dead lettering through exchange and routing policy; in Go, retain ownership, stop-path, and changed-case evidence.

Configure dead lettering through mutable policy where possible and verify source read, target write, exchange existence, binding, and route permissions; in Go, retain ownership, stop-path, and changed-case evidence.
Detect and bound dead-letter cycles using history, stable identity, attempt policy, route analysis, and a terminal parking destination; in Go, retain ownership, stop-path, and changed-case evidence.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: RabbitMQ and Go Commit to the mission model · Lab Dead Letter Trigger Route
steps.instruction:
lab case — A lab Go messaging team handles case 4410 during Go publisher supervisor losing one state notification. Build deterministic Go evidence to bound dead-letter history, stop cycles, and separate retry tiers from authorized parking; prove cleanup under a single deadline and cancellation cause; then fail the canary and prove older Go consumers remain compatible. Browser code uses pure values and state transitions only. amqp091-go, AMQP sockets, RabbitMQ, TLS, partitions, restarts, Docker, databases, race and leak checks, load, upgrades, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates.
